<title>Galois representations — the absolute Galois group in matrix form</title>

<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false,
      macros:{
        '\\Spec':'\\Spec',
        '\\Gal':'\\Gal',
        '\\Hom':'\\Hom',
        '\\tr':'\\tr',
        '\\ad':'\\ad',
        '\\ind':'\\ind'
      }
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  /* sticky sidebar TOC (wide screens only; narrow screens fall back to top-nav) */
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  /* callback-css-auto-begin */
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
/* callback-css-auto-end */
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>
<!-- display-prefs-head-auto-begin -->
<script src="./js/display-prefs.js"></script>
<!-- display-prefs-head-auto-end -->
</head>
rawBodyPrefix:
← Notebook
1 Absolute Galois group 2 Galois representations 3 Three families of examples 4 Tate module of an elliptic curve 5 Frobenius and traces 6 Local $L$-factors 7 Modular forms and Deligne 8 Semisimplification 9 Ramification and conductor

Galois representations

The absolute Galois group $G_\mathbb{Q}$ is unimaginably huge — but you can study it by letting it act on vector spaces. A Galois representation $\rho: G_\mathbb{Q} \to \mathrm{GL}_n$ packages an entire Diophantine object (an elliptic curve, a modular form, a number field) as a tower of matrices. The Frobenius trace at each unramified prime is the fingerprint.

Prerequisites: Frobenius and reciprocity, representation theory, Galois theory, elliptic curves, $p$-adic numbers.

blocks.html:
1. The absolute Galois group $G_\mathbb{Q}$

Let $\bar{\mathbb{Q}} \subset \mathbb{C}$ be the field of all algebraic numbers. The absolute Galois group of $\mathbb{Q}$ is

$$ G_\mathbb{Q} \;=\; \Gal(\bar{\mathbb{Q}}/\mathbb{Q}) \;=\; \{\sigma:\bar{\mathbb{Q}}\to\bar{\mathbb{Q}} \text{ field auto, fixing } \mathbb{Q}\}. $$

It is the symmetry group of all of algebraic number theory at once. Every finite Galois extension $K/\mathbb{Q}$ appears as a quotient:

$$ G_\mathbb{Q} \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \Gal(K/\mathbb{Q}). $$

And $G_\mathbb{Q}$ is the inverse limit over all such finite quotients — a profinite group:

$$ G_\mathbb{Q} \;\cong\; \varprojlim_{K/\mathbb{Q}\text{ finite Galois}}\; \Gal(K/\mathbb{Q}). $$

Its topology is generated by these finite quotients. Two automorphisms are close when they agree on a large finite extension. This topology is compact, Hausdorff, totally disconnected — like the Cantor set.

Frobenius at a prime $p$

For each prime $p$, pick an embedding $\bar{\mathbb{Q}} \hookrightarrow \bar{\mathbb{Q}}_p$. That choice identifies a decomposition subgroup $D_p \subset G_\mathbb{Q}$ — the stabiliser of the embedding. Inside $D_p$ sits the inertia subgroup $I_p$, which encodes wild/tame ramification. When $p$ is unramified in a finite Galois extension $K/\mathbb{Q}$, the quotient $D_p/I_p$ is cyclic, generated canonically by the Frobenius element

$$ \mathrm{Frob}_p \in \Gal(K/\mathbb{Q}) \big/ I_p, \qquad \mathrm{Frob}_p: x \mapsto x^p \pmod{\mathfrak p}. $$

Different choices of embedding give conjugate $\mathrm{Frob}_p$'s, so $\mathrm{Frob}_p$ is well-defined as a conjugacy class in $G_\mathbb{Q}/I_p$.

params.title: Widget 1 · Profinite cartoon of $G_\mathbb{Q}$
params.hint: finite quotients fan out; $G_\mathbb{Q}$ sits at the top

layout.content: $G_\mathbb{Q}$ surjects onto every finite Galois group $\Gal(K/\mathbb{Q})$. Reading the diagram downward is a tower of quotients; reading upward is the inverse limit.

blocks.html: Think of $G_\mathbb{Q}$ as "the group of all coherent permutations of algebraic numbers that respect every field relation." Its structure is almost entirely unknown — understanding its representations is a substitute for understanding the group itself.

2. What is a Galois representation?

A Galois representation is a continuous homomorphism

$$ \rho \colon G_\mathbb{Q} \;\longrightarrow\; \mathrm{GL}_n(K) $$

where the coefficient field $K$ is one of three standard choices:

coefficient fieldnamesource
$K = \mathbb{C}$Artin representationfinite image; factors through $\Gal(L/\mathbb{Q})$ for some finite $L$
$K = \bar{\mathbb{Q}}_\ell$$\ell$-adic representationfrom étale cohomology, elliptic curves, modular forms
$K = \bar{\mathbb{F}}_\ell$mod-$\ell$ representationreductions of $\ell$-adic representations; images are finite

Continuity is the real content. Since $G_\mathbb{Q}$ is profinite, a continuous $\rho$ must have open kernel on finite quotients, but an $\ell$-adic representation need not factor through any finite quotient — the target $\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{Z}_\ell)$ itself is profinite, and a generic element of its image can have infinite order.

Why "linear symmetries of $G_\mathbb{Q}$"?

A Galois representation is the $\ell$-adic (or complex) analog of a representation of a finite group. For finite groups, characters classify representations, and characters are determined by conjugacy classes. For $G_\mathbb{Q}$, the trace of Frobenius at each unramified prime plays the role of the character — and the astonishing fact is that this finite amount of data really does determine $\rho$ (up to semisimplification).

params.title: Widget 2 · The representation map

blocks.html:
See also
  • Field extensions and automorphisms
  • Group representations

Used in
Galois representations · Frobenius traces and compatibility
Galois representations · Irreducibility and semisimplification
Modularity and FLT · Deformation rings
Modularity and FLT · Modularity theorem

3. Three families of examples

(a) The cyclotomic character

The group $G_\mathbb{Q}$ acts on the $\ell^n$-th roots of unity $\mu_{\ell^n} = \{\zeta \in \bar{\mathbb{Q}}: \zeta^{\ell^n}=1\} \cong \mathbb{Z}/\ell^n$. On a generator $\zeta$, any $\sigma \in G_\mathbb{Q}$ acts by

$$ \sigma(\zeta) \;=\; \zeta^{\,\chi_n(\sigma)}, \qquad \chi_n(\sigma) \in (\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n)^\times. $$

These fit together into the $\ell$-adic cyclotomic character

$$ \chi \colon G_\mathbb{Q} \;\longrightarrow\; \varprojlim_n\, (\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n)^\times \;=\; \mathbb{Z}_\ell^\times. $$

This is a 1-dimensional $\ell$-adic Galois representation. At an unramified prime $p \neq \ell$, $\chi(\mathrm{Frob}_p) = p$.

params.title: Widget 3 · Cyclotomic action on $\mu_{\ell^n}$
params.hint: slide $n$; Galois elements act by multiplication in $(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n)^\times$

layout.html: n=2

blocks.html:
(b) Dirichlet characters

A Dirichlet character modulo $N$ is a homomorphism $\psi:(\mathbb{Z}/N)^\times \to \mathbb{C}^\times$. Composing with the mod-$N$ cyclotomic quotient $G_\mathbb{Q} \twoheadrightarrow (\mathbb{Z}/N)^\times$ gives a 1-dimensional Artin representation

$$ \rho_\psi \colon G_\mathbb{Q} \to (\mathbb{Z}/N)^\times \xrightarrow{\psi} \mathbb{C}^\times. $$

Every 1-dimensional Artin representation is of this form (class field theory for $\mathbb{Q}$). The image is finite, and $\rho_\psi(\mathrm{Frob}_p) = \psi(p)$ for $p \nmid N$.

(c) Artin representations

More generally, any representation $\Gal(L/\mathbb{Q}) \to \mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{C})$ of a finite Galois group inflates to a continuous $\rho: G_\mathbb{Q} \to \mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{C})$ with finite image. These are Artin representations, and their conjectural $L$-functions (the Artin conjecture) are at the heart of the Langlands program.

(d) The 2-dimensional zoo

The most interesting representations are 2-dimensional and $\ell$-adic, coming from:

  • Tate modules of elliptic curves (§4),
  • weight-2 cusp forms (§7),
  • odd irreducible Artin representations (Khare–Wintenberger theorem).

4. The Tate module of an elliptic curve

Let $E/\mathbb{Q}$ be an elliptic curve and $\ell$ a prime. The $\ell^n$-torsion subgroup $E[\ell^n] \subset E(\bar{\mathbb{Q}})$ is isomorphic to $(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n)^2$ as an abelian group, and $G_\mathbb{Q}$ acts on it because the torsion points have algebraic coordinates.

The multiplication-by-$\ell$ map $[\ell]: E[\ell^{n+1}] \to E[\ell^n]$ lets us take the inverse limit:

$$ T_\ell E \;=\; \varprojlim_n\, E[\ell^n] \;\cong\; \mathbb{Z}_\ell^2. $$

This is the Tate module. It is a free rank-2 $\mathbb{Z}_\ell$-module, and $G_\mathbb{Q}$ acts $\mathbb{Z}_\ell$-linearly. Choosing a basis gives the $\ell$-adic Galois representation attached to $E$:

$$ \rho_{E,\ell} \colon G_\mathbb{Q} \;\longrightarrow\; \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Z}_\ell). $$

Tensoring with $\mathbb{Q}_\ell$ gives a representation on a 2-dimensional $\mathbb{Q}_\ell$-vector space $V_\ell E = T_\ell E \otimes \mathbb{Q}_\ell$.

params.title: Widget 4 · The Tate-module tower $\cdots \to E[\ell^3] \to E[\ell^2] \to E[\ell]$
params.hint: click a level to see the $(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n)^2$ lattice it represents

blocks.html: The determinant of $\rho_{E,\ell}$ is the cyclotomic character $\chi$: that is, the Weil pairing identifies $\bigwedge^2 T_\ell E \cong \mathbb{Z}_\ell(1)$, the 1-twist of the trivial representation. So $\det \rho_{E,\ell}(\mathrm{Frob}_p) = p$.

5. Frobenius traces and the Hasse bound

Suppose $E/\mathbb{Q}$ has good reduction at $p$ — equivalently, $p$ does not divide the discriminant. Then $p$ is unramified in $\rho_{E,\ell}$ (for $\ell \neq p$), and $\rho_{E,\ell}(\mathrm{Frob}_p)$ is a well-defined conjugacy class in $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Z}_\ell)$. The matrix itself depends on choices, but its characteristic polynomial does not:

$$ \det(t\cdot I - \rho_{E,\ell}(\mathrm{Frob}_p)) \;=\; t^2 - a_p\, t + p, $$

where

$$ a_p \;=\; p + 1 - |\tilde E(\mathbb{F}_p)| $$

is the trace of Frobenius: count the points of the reduction $\tilde E$ over $\mathbb{F}_p$, and subtract from $p+1$. This is where Diophantine geometry meets linear algebra.

Hasse's theorem

The eigenvalues $\alpha_p, \beta_p$ of $\rho_{E,\ell}(\mathrm{Frob}_p)$ are algebraic integers satisfying $\alpha_p\beta_p = p$ and $|\alpha_p| = |\beta_p| = \sqrt{p}$. Equivalently,

$$ |a_p| \;=\; |\alpha_p + \beta_p| \;\le\; 2\sqrt{p}. $$

This is the Riemann hypothesis for elliptic curves over $\mathbb{F}_p$. The eigenvalues land on a circle of radius $\sqrt{p}$.

params.title: Widget 5 · The Frobenius matrix dial
params.hint: slide $a_p$ within $[-2\sqrt{p},\,2\sqrt{p}]$; eigenvalues move on a circle of radius $\sqrt{p}$

layout.html: a=-2

params.title: Widget 6 · $a_p$ bar chart for $E: y^2 = x^3 - x$, primes $p \le 100$
params.hint: bars obey the Hasse bound; half are zero (the CM symmetry)

params.title: Widget 7 · Hasse disk — eigenvalues of $\mathrm{Frob}_p$
params.hint: the $\sqrt{p}$-circle; conjugate eigenvalues as two dots

layout.html: a=-2

blocks.html:
See also
  • Frobenius elements

Used in
Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer · Birch–Swinnerton-Dyer heuristic
Galois representations · Inertia, ramification, and the conductor
Galois representations · Residual reps, deformations, modularity
Modularity and FLT · Deformation rings

6. Local $L$-factors from Galois representations

Once you have the representation $\rho_{E,\ell}$, the $L$-function of $E$ emerges by a very clean formula. For each unramified prime $p$ (not $\ell$), define the local $L$-factor

$$ L_p(E,s) \;=\; \det\!\Big(1 - \rho_{E,\ell}(\mathrm{Frob}_p)\, p^{-s}\Big)^{-1} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 - a_p p^{-s} + p^{1-2s}}. $$

This is literally the determinant of a $2\times 2$ matrix. The global $L$-function is the Euler product

$$ L(E,s) \;=\; \prod_p L_p(E,s). $$

For ramified primes (bad reduction) the Euler factor is $1/(1 - a_p p^{-s})$ with $a_p \in \{-1, 0, 1\}$ depending on the reduction type. The conductor $N$ of $E$ records the ramification.

What the determinant encodes

The local factor packages all the local information at $p$: how many points the reduction has, and whether reduction is good/multiplicative/additive. Different Galois representations $\rho$ give different $L$-functions — and conversely, $L(\rho,s)$ nearly determines $\rho$ (strong multiplicity one).

params.title: Widget 8 · Local $L$-factor from Frobenius characteristic polynomial
params.hint: for an elliptic curve, the matrix has characteristic polynomial $t^2 - a_p t + p$

layout.html: a=-2

blocks.html:
7. Modular forms and Deligne's construction

Let $f = \sum_{n\ge 1} a_n(f) q^n$ be a normalised cuspidal eigenform of weight $k\ge 2$ on $\Gamma_0(N)$ with character $\varepsilon$. Its Fourier coefficients $a_n(f)$ are algebraic numbers, generating a finite extension $K_f \subset \bar{\mathbb{Q}}$.

A theorem of Eichler–Shimura (weight 2) and Deligne (all weights) attaches to $f$, for each prime $\ell$ and each place $\lambda$ of $K_f$ above $\ell$, a continuous 2-dimensional Galois representation

$$ \rho_{f,\lambda} \colon G_\mathbb{Q} \;\longrightarrow\; \mathrm{GL}_2(K_{f,\lambda}) $$

that is unramified outside $N\ell$ and satisfies, for every prime $p \nmid N\ell$,

$$ \tr\rho_{f,\lambda}(\mathrm{Frob}_p) \;=\; a_p(f), \qquad \det\rho_{f,\lambda}(\mathrm{Frob}_p) \;=\; \varepsilon(p)\, p^{k-1}. $$

The Fourier coefficients of an eigenform are traces of Frobenius. This is one of the deepest bridges in number theory: automorphic $\leftrightarrow$ Galois.

Weight 2 and elliptic curves

When $f$ has weight 2 and rational coefficients, $\rho_{f,\ell}$ lives in $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Z}_\ell)$. Comparing $\tr\rho_{f,\ell}(\mathrm{Frob}_p) = a_p(f)$ to the $a_p$ of an elliptic curve, one is led to ask whether every elliptic curve arises this way. That question has an answer — but you'll find it in its own discussion, not here.

params.title: Widget 9 · Eigenform $\to$ Galois representation

blocks.html:
The picture in §7 closes the loop: start with a Diophantine object, attach a Galois representation, read off traces of Frobenius, recover the object's arithmetic. This is the modern formulation of "class field theory for non-abelian extensions."

What comes next

Everything downstream — the Langlands program, modularity, $R=T$ theorems, deformation theory of Galois representations — assumes this framework. The next layers of the Notebook explore $L$-functions explicitly (see Dirichlet series and Euler products) and the arithmetic of specific representations (see modular forms).

See also
  • Modularity theorem

Used in
Modularity and FLT · Ribet's level lowering

8. Irreducibility and semisimplification

A Galois representation $\rho: G_\mathbb{Q} \to \mathrm{GL}_n(K)$ is irreducible if $K^n$ has no proper non-zero $G_\mathbb{Q}$-stable subspace, and semisimple if it decomposes as a direct sum of irreducibles. As with finite-group representations, not every representation is semisimple — an upper-triangular $\rho = \begin{pmatrix}\chi_1 & *\\ 0 & \chi_2\end{pmatrix}$ with non-zero extension class is reducible but not a direct sum. The semisimplification $\rho^{\mathrm{ss}} = \chi_1 \oplus \chi_2$ is obtained by throwing away the off-diagonal cocycle.

Because traces are additive on short exact sequences, $\tr\rho(\mathrm{Frob}_p) = \tr\rho^{\mathrm{ss}}(\mathrm{Frob}_p)$ at every unramified prime. So Frobenius traces only ever see the semisimplification. By the Brauer–Nesbitt / Chebotarev combination, two semisimple $\ell$-adic representations of $G_\mathbb{Q}$ are isomorphic iff their traces of Frobenius agree at a density-one set of primes — a dramatic rigidity statement. For $\rho_{E,\ell}$ from an elliptic curve, a theorem of Serre guarantees irreducibility for all but finitely many $\ell$, so the semisimplification is just $\rho_{E,\ell}$ itself.

See also
  • Schur's lemma

9. Inertia, ramification, and the conductor

Fix a prime $p$ and an embedding $\bar{\mathbb{Q}} \hookrightarrow \bar{\mathbb{Q}}_p$. Inside the decomposition group $D_p \subset G_\mathbb{Q}$ sits the inertia subgroup $I_p$, the kernel of $D_p \twoheadrightarrow \Gal(\bar{\mathbb{F}}_p/\mathbb{F}_p)$. A representation $\rho: G_\mathbb{Q} \to \mathrm{GL}_n(K)$ is unramified at $p$ when $\rho(I_p) = 1$; only in that case does $\rho(\mathrm{Frob}_p)$ make sense as a well-defined conjugacy class, so the Frobenius machinery of §5 only applies at unramified primes.

For the bad primes, the Artin conductor exponent $f_p(\rho) = \dim(V) - \dim(V^{I_p}) + \text{(wild term)}$ quantifies how badly $\rho$ is ramified at $p$: the first piece counts missing inertia invariants (the tame drop), the wild term measures higher ramification. The global conductor is the product $N(\rho) = \prod_p p^{f_p(\rho)}$, supported on the finitely many ramified primes. For an elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$ the conductor $N_E$ satisfies $f_p = 1$ at primes of multiplicative reduction and $f_p \ge 2$ at primes of additive reduction, matching the conductor that appears in the $L$-function of $E$.

See also
  • p-adic norm and completion
